turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Breyer has the opinion of the Court in two cases this morning.

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: The first one is Howell versus Howell. It's about military veterans who receive retirement pay as most do after they leave the Armed Forces after a certain number of years. The disabled veterans can receive disability benefits from the government. So if a veteran is entitled to both, then he typically has to waive some of the retirement pay in order to get the disability benefits which you likely do because the benefits come tax free, but the retirement pay does not. So, this case comes out of divorce of John and Sandra Howell who divorced in 1991. John was a veteran. He planned to retire from the Air Force the following year. And the Arizona courts when they were divorced thought that John's anticipated military retirement pay was community property and so they divided it 50:50 between the two, but then more than a decade later, the VA found that John was 20% disabled and he became eligible for disability benefits but to obtain them he had to and he did waive about $250.00 worth of his total 1,500 of military retirement pay. So he got the money back in the form of disability pay but Sandra didn't and she lost her share of her retirement pay. It was reduced by half of what John had to get paid back to get the disability pay. The Arizona courts ordered John to make up the difference. They said John has to ensure that Sandra received her full 50% of the military retirement without regard for the disability. How can Arizona do that? Well, it might seem as if they could but long ago in a case called McCarty, this Court held that states could not consider military retirement pay to be community property and they couldn't divide military retirement pay between divorcing spouses. Then Congress changed the law and it said they could but with an exception. The exception consisted of that part of the retirement pay that the retired veteran waived in order to get disability pay. In a case called Mansell which came along after, we said Congress meant what it said. States cannot treat the disability related part of the retirement pay as community property. They can't divide that part of it. As far as we've been able to decide Mansell governs here and the state did just what it says they can't do. It took the waived part and it divided that 50:50. The states says “Well, here John waived his retirement pay after the divorce, not at the time of divorce to get the disability,” but we just don't see why that matters. When John's military retirement pay came to Sandra, but at that time it was subject to John later deciding to waive a part of it. The state court did not extinguish and we doubt that it could have that future contingency. The state also said,” Well, what we are doing is ordering indemnification, not splitting the pay,” but that word as we point out is a semantic. It shows a semantic difference and nothing more than that. We know that family court when it first determines the value of a family's assets remains free to take account but this particular asset is subject to a contingency so might later be waived and it could take accountable reductions in value when it calculates spousal support, but that isn't what happened here. So for these and other reasons set forth in our opinion, we reverse the judgment of the Arizona Supreme Court and remand the case. Justice Thomas has filed an opinion concurring in part and concurring in the judgment. Justice Gorsuch took no part in the consideration or decision of the case.
